content:
# Luke Chapter 15 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 107-108 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_015_page_001.jpg, LUK_015_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And all the tax-collectors and the sinners were drawing-near to him to hear him.

**2.** And the Pharisees and the scribes were murmuring, saying, "This [man] receives sinners and eats-with them."

**3.** And he spoke to them this parable, saying,

**4.** "What man of you, having a hundred sheep and having lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the wilderness and go after the lost [one] until he finds it?

**5.** And having found [it], he lays [it] upon his shoulders, rejoicing.

**6.** And having come into the house, he calls-together the friends and the neighbors, saying to them, 'Rejoice with me, because I have found my sheep, the lost [one].'

**7.** I say to you that thus there shall be joy in heaven over one sinner repenting, [more] than over ninety-nine righteous [ones] who have no need of repentance.

**8.** Or what woman, having ten drachmas, if she should lose one drachma, does not light a lamp and sweep the house and seek carefully until she finds [it]?

**9.** And having found [it], she calls-together the friends and the neighbors, saying, 'Rejoice with me, because I have found the drachma which I lost.'

**10.** Thus, I say to you, there comes-to-be joy before the angels of God over one sinner repenting."

**11.** And he said, "A certain man had two sons.

**12.** And the younger of them said to the father, 'Father, give to me the portion of the property falling-to [me].' And he divided to them the livelihood.

**13.** And after not many days, the younger son, having gathered-together all [things], traveled-away into a far country, and there he squandered his property, living dissolutely.

**14.** And when he had spent all [things], a severe famine came-to-be throughout that country, and he himself began to be-in-want.

**15.** And having gone, he joined-himself to one of the citizens of that country, and he sent him into his fields to feed pigs.

**16.** And he was desiring to fill his belly from the carob-pods which the pigs were eating, and no one was giving [anything] to him.

**17.** And having come to himself, he said, 'How many hired-servants of my father have abundance of bread, but I am perishing here with famine!

**18.** Having arisen, I will go to my father, and I will say to him, "Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you;

**19.** I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me as one of your hired-servants."'

**20.** And having arisen, he came to his own father. And while he was still far-off, his father saw him and was moved-with-compassion, and having run, he fell upon his neck and kissed him.

**21.** And the son said to him, 'Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you; I am no longer worthy to be called your son.'

**22.** But the father said to his servants, 'Bring out the first robe and clothe him, and give a ring for his hand and sandals for [his] feet.

**23.** And bring the fattened calf, slaughter [it], and having eaten, let us be-merry;

**24.** because this son of mine was dead and has come-to-life-again, was lost and has been found.' And they began to be-merry.

**25.** And his elder son was in [the] field; and as he came [and] drew-near to the house, he heard music and dancing.

**26.** And having called-to [him] one of the servants, he was inquiring what these [things] might be.

**27.** And he said to him, 'Your brother has come, and your father has slaughtered the fattened calf, because he received him back in-good-health.'

**28.** And he was angry and was not willing to go in. And his father, having come out, was urging him.

**29.** But answering, he said to the father, 'Behold, so many years I am serving you, and never did I transgress a command of yours, and to me you never gave a young-goat so that I might be-merry with my friends.

**30.** But when this son of yours came, the [one] having devoured your livelihood with prostitutes, you slaughtered for him the fattened calf.'

**31.** And he said to him, 'Child, you are always with me, and all my [things] are yours.

**32.** But it was necessary to be-merry and to rejoice, because this brother of yours was dead and has come-to-life, and was lost and has been found.'"

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **15:1 — "all the tax-collectors and the sinners" (παντες οι τελωναι και οι αμαρτωλοι):** Sinaiticus reads παντες οι τελωναι και οι αμαρτωλοι, with the emphatic παντες ("all") — not merely some, but the entire class of those deemed socially and religiously unclean. The article before both nouns (οι τελωναι...οι αμαρτωλοι) identifies them as recognized categories: the "tax-collectors" (τελωναι) were Jews who contracted with Rome to collect taxes and were despised as collaborators and extortioners; the "sinners" (αμαρτωλοι) were those whose occupations or lifestyles placed them outside the boundaries of Torah observance as defined by Pharisaic interpretation. The imperfect ησαν...εγγιζοντες ("were drawing-near") is periphrastic, indicating a continuous, habitual pattern — these people kept coming to Jesus, repeatedly.

2. **15:2 — "murmuring" (διεγογγυζον):** Sinaiticus reads διεγογγυζον, imperfect active of διαγογγυζω, a compound verb (δια + γογγυζω) intensifying the simple γογγυζω ("to grumble"). The prefix δια- suggests the murmuring was spreading through the group — a pervasive, shared complaint. The word deliberately echoes the LXX usage for Israel's murmuring against Moses in the wilderness (Exodus 15:24; 16:2, 7-8; Numbers 14:2, 27, 36), placing the Pharisees and scribes in the role of the rebellious Israelites who opposed God's chosen deliverer. The present participle λεγοντες ("saying") introduces their complaint: "This [man] receives sinners and eats-with them" (ουτος αμαρτωλους προσδεχεται και συνεσθιει αυτοις). The verb προσδεχεται ("receives/welcomes") implies deliberate acceptance, not passive tolerance; συνεσθιει ("eats-with") violates the purity codes governing table-fellowship, which functioned as the primary social boundary marker in Second Temple Judaism.

3. **15:3 — "this parable" (την παραβολην ταυτην):** Sinaiticus reads την παραβολην ταυτην, singular — "this parable." Luke presents all three stories (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son) as a single composite parable, not three separate ones. The singular form signals that the three narratives function as one unified response to the Pharisees' complaint in verse 2. Each story intensifies: from one sheep out of a hundred (1%), to one coin out of ten (10%), to one son out of two (50%) — the proportion of loss increases, and with it the emotional investment.

4. **15:4 — "having a hundred sheep" (εχων εκατον προβατα):** Sinaiticus reads εχων εκατον προβατα. A flock of one hundred sheep represents moderate prosperity — not wealthy by Palestinian standards, but a substantial holding requiring dedicated shepherding. The question τις ανθρωπος εξ υμων ("what man of you") draws the Pharisees into the story, forcing them to identify with the shepherd's logic. The verb καταλειπει ("leaves") is present indicative — it describes what anyone naturally does, not what one should do. The phrase εν τη ερημω ("in the wilderness") is significant: the ninety-nine are left not in safety but in the open wilderness, unprotected, while the shepherd pursues the one. This detail, often overlooked, underscores the radical priority of seeking the lost — the shepherd risks the many for the one.

5. **15:5 — "upon his shoulders" (επι τους ωμους αυτου):** Sinaiticus reads επι τους ωμους αυτου. The image of the shepherd carrying the sheep on his shoulders is ancient and widespread — it appears in Psalm 28:9 LXX (the Lord carrying his people), in Isaiah 40:11 (God gathering lambs in his arms), and was a common motif in Greco-Roman art (the kriophoros, or ram-bearer). The participle χαιρων ("rejoicing") modifies the entire action of carrying — the shepherd's journey home is characterized not by resentment at the sheep's wandering or exhaustion from the search, but by joy. The sheep does nothing to find its way back; it is entirely passive, found and carried.

6. **15:6 — "calls-together the friends and the neighbors" (συγκαλει τους φιλους και τους γειτονας):** Sinaiticus reads συγκαλει τους φιλους και τους γειτονας. The verb συγκαλει ("calls-together") is a compound (συν + καλεω) indicating a deliberate gathering — the shepherd summons a community to share his joy. The imperative συγχαρητε μοι ("rejoice with me") is the key command of the parable: joy over recovery is communal, not private. This detail prepares for verse 7, where the community of heaven itself rejoices over one repenting sinner. The phrase "my sheep, the lost [one]" (το προβατον μου το απολωλος) uses the perfect participle απολωλος — the sheep's identity as "the lost one" has become its defining characteristic, now reversed by finding.

7. **15:7 — "joy in heaven" (χαρα εν τω ουρανω):** Sinaiticus reads χαρα εν τω ουρανω εσται — "joy in heaven shall be." The circumlocution "in heaven" avoids direct naming of God while clearly identifying the divine realm as the locus of rejoicing. The comparative construction — "more than over ninety-nine righteous [ones] who have no need of repentance" (η επι ενενηκοντα εννεα δικαιοις οιτινες ου χρειαν εχουσιν μετανοιας) — is deeply ironic in context: the Pharisees consider themselves the righteous ninety-nine, but Jesus' framing exposes that heaven's priorities are inverted from theirs. The word μετανοια ("repentance") literally means "change of mind" (μετα + νοια from νους), denoting a fundamental reorientation of thought, will, and direction.

8. **15:8 — "ten drachmas" (δραχμας δεκα):** Sinaiticus reads δραχμας δεκα. The drachma was a Greek silver coin roughly equivalent to the Roman denarius — approximately one day's wage for a laborer. Ten drachmas may represent the woman's entire savings, or possibly coins from a headdress ornament (a traditional wedding gift in Palestinian culture), making the loss not merely financial but deeply personal. The three actions — "light a lamp" (απτει λυχνον), "sweep the house" (σαροι την οικιαν), "seek carefully" (ζητει επιμελως) — reflect the reality of searching in a windowless Palestinian peasant home with a dirt floor where a small coin could easily disappear into cracks or debris.

9. **15:8-9 — The woman as divine image:** This parable is remarkable for its use of a woman as the figure representing God's seeking love — paired with the male shepherd in verses 4-7, the female searcher completes a gender-inclusive portrait of divine persistence. The verb συγκαλει ("calls-together") and the phrase τας φιλας και γειτονας ("the friends and neighbors") use feminine forms in verse 9 — the woman gathers her circle of women friends, mirroring the shepherd's male circle in verse 6. The parallelism is precise and deliberate.

10. **15:10 — "joy before the angels of God" (χαρα...ενωπιον των αγγελων του θεου):** Sinaiticus reads χαρα γινεται ενωπιον των αγγελων του θεου — "joy comes-to-be before the angels of God." The preposition ενωπιον ("before/in the presence of") is significant: the joy is not said to be the angels' joy, but joy that occurs in their presence — that is, God's own joy, witnessed by the angelic court. This subtle distinction preserves the theological point that it is God himself who rejoices over the repentant sinner, with the angels as witnesses to divine joy.

11. **15:12 — "the portion of the property falling-to [me]" (το επιβαλλον μερος της ουσιας):** Sinaiticus reads το επιβαλλον μερος της ουσιας. The participle επιβαλλον ("falling-to/belonging-to") is a legal-technical term for the share of inheritance due to an heir. Under Jewish law (Deuteronomy 21:17; cf. Mishnah Baba Bathra 8:5), the elder son received a double portion — two-thirds of the estate — and the younger received one-third. However, the father normally retained use and income from the property during his lifetime; the son's request to receive his share immediately was an extraordinary act tantamount to treating his father as already dead. The phrase "he divided to them the livelihood" (διειλεν αυτοις τον βιον) uses βιος ("livelihood/means of living"), distinct from ζωη ("life") — emphasizing that what was divided was not merely property but the father's very means of sustenance.

12. **15:13 — "living dissolutely" (ζων ασωτως):** Sinaiticus reads ζων ασωτως. The adverb ασωτως derives from α-σωτος ("unsaved/without salvation"), literally "without hope of saving" — hence "dissolutely, wastefully, beyond recovery." The cognate noun ασωτια ("dissipation") appears in Ephesians 5:18 and Titus 1:6; 1 Peter 4:4. The verb διεσκορπισεν ("he squandered/scattered") is the same word used for scattering seed (Matthew 25:24) and scattering sheep (Matthew 26:31) — his wealth was dispersed with reckless abandon, like seed thrown on every kind of ground. The "far country" (χωραν μακραν) emphasizes total separation from the father — geographic distance as spiritual metaphor.

13. **15:14 — "a severe famine" (λιμος ισχυρα):** Sinaiticus reads λιμος ισχυρα. The adjective ισχυρα ("severe/strong") intensifies the catastrophe. The convergence of personal destitution (having spent all) and external crisis (famine) creates a double bind — there is nothing left within and nothing available without. The verb υστερεισθαι ("to be-in-want") is present middle/passive infinitive — he began to experience ongoing, sustained deprivation. Famines were common in the ancient Mediterranean world and could reduce entire populations to desperation; for a foreign Jew with no social network, the situation would be particularly dire.

14. **15:15 — "to feed pigs" (βοσκειν χοιρους):** Sinaiticus reads βοσκειν χοιρους. For a Jewish audience, this detail represents the absolute nadir of degradation. Pigs were unclean animals (Leviticus 11:7; Deuteronomy 14:8), and contact with them rendered a person ritually impure. The Talmud (Baba Kamma 82b) states: "Cursed is the man who raises pigs." The verb εκολληθη ("he joined-himself/attached-himself") is a strong term — the same word used for a man "cleaving" to his wife (Matthew 19:5; Genesis 2:24 LXX) — suggesting a desperate, clinging attachment born of sheer need. He who had demanded independence from his father now clings to a stranger for survival.

15. **15:16 — "carob-pods" (κερατιων):** Sinaiticus reads κερατιων, genitive plural of κερατιον — the pods of the carob tree (Ceratonia siliqua), also called "St. John's bread" from the later tradition that John the Baptist ate them. The carob pod is a dark, leathery fruit, mildly sweet, used primarily as animal fodder but also consumed by the very poor. The phrase "he was desiring to fill his belly" (επεθυμει γεμισαι την κοιλιαν αυτου) uses επεθυμει, imperfect of επιθυμεω ("to desire earnestly") — a sustained, unfulfilled longing. The devastating conclusion — "no one was giving [anything] to him" (ουδεις εδιδου αυτω) — expresses total abandonment: not even pig-food was offered to him. He is below the pigs in the social order.

16. **15:17 — "having come to himself" (εις εαυτον ελθων):** Sinaiticus reads εις εαυτον δε ελθων — "and having come to himself." This phrase, common in Greek literature (Epictetus, Discourses 3.1.15; Diodorus Siculus 13.95.2), denotes the recovery of rational self-awareness after a period of madness or delusion. The prodigal "comes to himself" — he was, in a sense, out of his mind during his dissolute living, and now returns to sanity. His first rational thought is comparative: "How many hired-servants (μισθιοι) of my father have abundance (περισσευονται) of bread" — even the lowest-status workers in his father's household have more than enough, while he, a son, is perishing (εγω δε λιμω ωδε απολλυμαι). The verb απολλυμαι ("I am perishing") echoes the "lost" (απολωλος) language of the sheep and coin parables.

17. **15:18-19 — The rehearsed speech:** Sinaiticus preserves the son's carefully planned speech: "Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you; I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me as one of your hired-servants." The phrase "against heaven" (εις τον ουρανον) is a Jewish circumlocution for "against God" (cf. Daniel 4:26; 1 Maccabees 3:18-19). The son's sin is thus doubly directed — vertically toward God and horizontally toward his father. The request to be made "as one of your hired-servants" (ως ενα των μισθιων σου) acknowledges that he has forfeited sonship and seeks the lowest possible restoration — not as a household servant (δουλος, who had permanent status), but as a hired laborer (μισθιος, who worked day by day with no security).

18. **15:20 — "was moved-with-compassion" (εσπλαγχνισθη):** Sinaiticus reads εσπλαγχνισθη, aorist passive of σπλαγχνιζομαι, derived from σπλαγχνα ("intestines/bowels") — literally, his inner organs were wrenched with compassion. This verb in the Gospels is used exclusively of Jesus (Matthew 9:36; 14:14; 15:32; 20:34; Mark 1:41; 6:34; 8:2; Luke 7:13) or of God-figures in parables (Matthew 18:27; here). The father sees the son "while he was still far-off" (ετι αυτου μακραν απεχοντος) — implying the father had been watching, perhaps daily, for his return. The sequence of four verbs — "saw" (ειδεν), "was moved-with-compassion" (εσπλαγχνισθη), "having run" (δραμων), "fell upon his neck" (επεπεσεν επι τον τραχηλον αυτου) — moves from perception to emotion to physical action. For an elderly patriarch to run was culturally shocking — men of dignity did not run (cf. Sirach 19:30). The father abandons dignity for love. The verb κατεφιλησεν ("kissed") is a compound (κατα + φιλεω) intensifying the simple verb — "kissed fervently/repeatedly."

19. **15:21 — The truncated speech:** Sinaiticus reads the son beginning his rehearsed speech — "Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you; I am no longer worthy to be called your son" — but the third line ("make me as one of your hired-servants") is absent. The father interrupts before the son can demote himself. The son is not allowed to negotiate his status downward; the father has already determined restoration. This interruption is the theological center of the parable: repentance is necessary but does not earn or negotiate the terms of acceptance. Grace precedes and overwhelms the penitent's proposals.

20. **15:22 — "the first robe" (στολην την πρωτην):** Sinaiticus reads στολην την πρωτην — "the first/best robe." The στολη is a long, flowing garment of distinction (cf. Mark 12:38; 16:5; Luke 20:46; Revelation 6:11; 7:9) — not an ordinary tunic but a robe of honor. The adjective πρωτην ("first") means either "the finest" (superlative sense) or "the former one" — possibly his own robe from before his departure. The three gifts — robe, ring (δακτυλιον), and sandals (υποδηματα) — are not mere clothing but symbols of restored status: the robe signifies honor and dignity; the ring signifies authority (cf. Genesis 41:42, Pharaoh's signet ring to Joseph; Esther 3:10; 8:2); the sandals signify free status, as slaves went barefoot. The father does not restore the son as a hired servant or even as a subordinate son — he restores him with full honor.

21. **15:23 — "the fattened calf" (τον μοσχον τον σιτευτον):** Sinaiticus reads τον μοσχον τον σιτευτον — the calf (μοσχος) that has been fattened (σιτευτος, from σιτευω, "to feed with grain"). The doubled article (τον...τον) indicates a specific, designated animal — "the" fattened calf, not merely any calf. A grain-fed calf was kept for special occasions; its slaughter represents extraordinary celebration. The subjunctive φαγοντες ευφρανθωμεν ("having eaten, let us be-merry") echoes the language of festive celebration throughout the LXX (cf. Deuteronomy 12:7; 14:26; 27:7; Isaiah 22:13).

22. **15:24 — "was dead and has come-to-life-again" (νεκρος ην και ανεζησεν):** Sinaiticus reads νεκρος ην και ανεζησεν — literally "dead he was and he lived-again." The verb ανεζησεν (aorist of αναζαω, "to live again") uses the prefix ανα- ("again/up"), the same prefix in αναστασις ("resurrection"). The father interprets the son's return in the most extreme categories available: not merely absent but dead, not merely returned but resurrected. The parallel "was lost and has been found" (απολωλως ην και ευρεθη) uses the perfect participle απολωλως, linking this narrative back to the lost sheep (verse 6) and lost coin (verse 9) — the same vocabulary of loss and finding unifies all three parables.

23. **15:25 — "music and dancing" (συμφωνιας και χορων):** Sinaiticus reads συμφωνιας και χορων. The word συμφωνια (from which "symphony" derives) literally means "sounding together" — instrumental music played in harmony. The χοροι ("dances/choral dances") indicate a celebration of communal proportions, not merely a private meal. The elder son encounters this from outside — he hears the party before he sees it, and must ask a servant for explanation. His separation from the household's joy is physical before it becomes emotional and spiritual.

24. **15:27 — "received him back in-good-health" (υγιαινοντα αυτον απελαβεν):** Sinaiticus reads υγιαινοντα αυτον απελαβεν. The participle υγιαινοντα ("being-in-good-health") comes from υγιαινω (from which "hygiene" derives) — the servant emphasizes that the younger son returned physically sound, not maimed or diseased. The verb απελαβεν (aorist of απολαμβανω, "to receive back") echoes the language of recovering something rightfully one's own. The servant's report is factual and neutral — "your brother has come" (ο αδελφος σου ηκει) — but the elder son will refuse this relational identification in verse 30.

25. **15:28 — "was angry and was not willing to go in" (ωργισθη και ουκ ηθελεν εισελθειν):** Sinaiticus reads ωργισθη και ουκ ηθελεν εισελθειν. The aorist ωργισθη ("he was angry") indicates a decisive burst of anger; the imperfect ηθελεν ("was willing") indicates a sustained refusal — his anger flared instantly, and his unwillingness continued. The father's response mirrors his response to the younger son: he "came out" (εξελθων) — just as he ran out to meet the prodigal, he now comes out to meet the elder. The verb παρεκαλει ("was urging/entreating") is imperfect — continuous, patient persuasion. The father humbles himself before both sons.

26. **15:29 — "so many years I am serving you" (τοσαυτα ετη δουλευω σοι):** Sinaiticus reads τοσαυτα ετη δουλευω σοι. The verb δουλευω ("I serve/am a slave to") is devastating in this context — the elder son describes his relationship with his father in terms of servitude, not sonship. He has lived in the father's house but with the mentality of a hired servant, the very status the younger son was willing to accept. The phrase "never did I transgress a command of yours" (ουδεποτε εντολην σου παρηλθον) uses παρηλθον (aorist of παρερχομαι, "to pass by/transgress"), the same verb used for "passing by" the law (16:17; Matthew 5:18). His obedience is framed in negative terms — an absence of transgression rather than a presence of love.

27. **15:30 — "this son of yours" (ο υιος σου ουτος):** Sinaiticus reads ο υιος σου ουτος — "this son of yours." The elder brother refuses to say "my brother," instead distancing himself with the contemptuous demonstrative ουτος ("this one"). He also introduces a detail absent from the narrative: "having devoured your livelihood with prostitutes" (ο καταφαγων σου τον βιον μετα πορνων). The narrator said only that the younger son lived "dissolutely" (ασωτως, verse 13); the elder brother specifies prostitutes — either from actual knowledge, malicious assumption, or self-righteous embellishment. The verb καταφαγων (aorist participle of κατεσθιω, "to devour/consume utterly") is more violent than the narrator's διεσκορπισεν ("squandered") — the elder brother maximizes the offense.

28. **15:31 — "Child" (τεκνον):** Sinaiticus reads τεκνον — "child," a term of intimate endearment, not the more formal υιε ("son"). The father's response is gentle despite the son's outburst. The affirmation "you are always with me, and all my [things] are yours" (συ παντοτε μετ εμου ει, και παντα τα εμα σα εστιν) is both reassurance and gentle rebuke — the elder son has always had access to everything, including the young goat he claims was never given. His deprivation is self-imposed, born of a servile mentality rather than actual want. The father's "all my things are yours" echoes the divine covenant language — everything the Father has belongs to his children.

29. **15:32 — "it was necessary" (εδει):** Sinaiticus reads ευφρανθηναι δε και χαρηναι εδει — "but to be-merry and to rejoice was necessary." The impersonal verb εδει (imperfect of δει, "it is necessary") indicates divine necessity — this is not merely a preference but a moral and theological imperative. Joy over the lost one's return is not optional but obligatory. The father's final words echo verse 24 exactly: "this brother of yours was dead and has come-to-life, and was lost and has been found" (ο αδελφος σου ουτος νεκρος ην και εζησεν, και απολωλως και ευρεθη). But where the father said "this son of mine" (ο υιος μου ουτος) in verse 24, he now says "this brother of yours" (ο αδελφος σου ουτος) — gently correcting the elder son's refusal to acknowledge brotherhood. The parable ends without resolution: we do not know whether the elder son enters the feast. The open ending is directed at the Pharisees and scribes of verse 2 — the invitation remains extended, the door remains open, but the choice to enter is theirs.

30. **15:1-32 — Structural unity of the three parables:** The three parables form a carefully constructed literary triptych responding to the single charge of verse 2. The pattern intensifies: the shepherd has 100 sheep and loses 1 (1%); the woman has 10 coins and loses 1 (10%); the father has 2 sons and loses 1 (50%). The search also intensifies: the shepherd seeks actively; the woman seeks more intensely (lighting, sweeping, searching carefully); the father does not seek at all but watches and waits — because a person, unlike a sheep or coin, must choose to return. All three parables end with communal celebration, but only the third introduces a figure who refuses to rejoice — the elder brother, who represents the Pharisees and scribes. The theological argument is complete: if heaven rejoices over one repentant sinner, those who refuse to share that joy have placed themselves outside the celebration of God.
